data.content_ref: inline:{"claim":"A rapid shift to cooler, wetter climate around 1190‑1150 BCE caused agricultural system collapse in the Eastern Mediterranean.","mechanism":"Hydroclimatic regime shift leading to flood‑induced soil erosion and reduced yields.","scope":{"covers":["Mycenaean Greece","Hittite Anatolia","Ugarit","Levantine city‑states"],"excludes":["Egypt"]},"forbidden":[{"case":"If high‑resolution speleothem records from the Aegean show sustained aridity rather than increased precipitation during 1190‑1150 BCE","eval":"rubric:std-hist"}],"prose_notes":"Interdisciplinary synthesis of paleoclimatic proxies (lake cores, pollen) suggests an anomalous wet phase that would stress flood‑prone agriculture, diverging from the drought narrative in neighbouring artefacts."}
